description: A consideration offered to support a claim, choice, evaluation, or action as fitting or warranted. A reason answers why something should be accepted or done in the context of deliberation. It is distinct from a physical cause, an intended purpose, a private motivation, and evidence, although any of those may also serve as a reason when explicitly offered in support.
sound_symbolism: Connecting 're' establishes that a reason is relational: it is a reason for something. Inward 'mo' gathers reflection, and final 'tha' gives the support a deliberate foundation that can be inspected rather than merely asserted.
grammatical_notes: A countable noun. Possessive order states what the reason supports: 'sherewa remotha' (a reason for the claim). As a clause participant: 'ha remotha ha sherewa panoru.' (This reason supports this claim.) Distinct from 'ka' (cause), 'porua' (purpose), 'thueli' (intend), and 'thesori' (evidence).

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Reasons remain considerations offered within conditions and perspectives, not permanent possessions that make a speaker infallible.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Speakers can request and examine reasons while keeping the person who offers them distinct from the claim under discussion.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: A reason may arise from practical experience and communal memory as well as abstract analysis, provided its supporting role is made clear.

tags.cognition: a consideration that supports acceptance or action
tags.dialogue: a ground offered so others can examine a claim or choice
tags.wisdom: deliberative support whose relevance and limits can be considered
